chunks.text: On March 12, 1930, the Indian leader Mohandas Gandhi, with seventy - eight companions
began to walk toward the coast of Dandi.
The distance was three hundred eighty kilometers.
The journey would last twenty four days.
Along the way, more and more people joined, and the march grew to thousands.
To make salt at the seashore.
At the time, the British colonial rule had forbidden Indian people from making salt themselves
holding it as a British monopoly.
The salt tax weighed most heavily on the poorest.
The moment Gandhi picked up a handful of salt from the sand
British rule was quietly, yet deeply shaken.
This was a thorough, nonviolent protest, carrying no weapons.
Gandhi was imprisoned many times by the British.
But his nonviolent movement eventually led to Indian independence and inspired movements throughout the world.
They, too, began to walk after listening to the footsteps of Gandhi.
